RAVI MEHTA
Ci Al II ‘l A
FAIR
POLITICAL
PRACTICES
COMMISSION
June 4,
1996
Roger A.
Brown
38 North Washington Street
P.O.
Box 475
Sonora,
CA
95370
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—96-155
Dear Mr.
Brown:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Susan Larson regarding her responsibilities as the
Director of Planning for Calaveras County under the provisions of
the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
QUESTIONS
1.
Does the definition of “gift” in the Act include gifts
received by the director in a bona fide dating relationship for
purposes of the Act’s confliát’of interest disclosure and
disqualification provisions and the gift limits of the Act?
2.
If the director shares
a residence with her fiancee and
shares expenses for food,
shelter, electricity and the like,
and
pays these expenses from a joint checking account into which both
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000-18995.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


Our File No. A9
155
Page
2
fldividual
deposit their earnings
would this
trigg
any diSclOSur
requir
Of the director?
USIONS
1.
Gif exchanged in a bona fide dating relationSh.
are
not gif
8 for purposes of the Act’s conflict of interest
the Act?
disclosur
and dasgualf
Provision
5 and the gift limits o
2.
The fact that the director shares a residenc
with her
fiancee and shares expenses for food,
shelter
electricit
and
pays these expenses from a joint checking account into Which both
individual
deposit their earnings would not result in any
disclosure reguire
for the director.
Ms.
Larson is the Director of Planning for Calaveras County.
Ms.
Larson has recently become engag and has accepted an
does business in the county.
engagem ring from her fiancee
Ralph Servidio2
Mr
Servidio
In additi
0
the director currently shares a residence with
her fiancee and shares expenses for food,
shelter
electricity and
the like
These expenses are paid from a joint checking account
into Which both individuals depos their earnings
Gifts
all
IS
The Act provides a variety of Obligatio
5 on Public officials
in connectio
with the receipt of gifts
o
First,
every public official must disclose all his or her
economic interests that could oreseeably be affected by the
exercise of the Official,
duties.
(Sections 8
1002(c)
872Q
87313)
A
HpUbli
offcjal, is defined broadly to include every
natural person who is a member, Officer
employ
or consultant
of a State or local overnme agency
(Section 82048; Regula
0
18700.)
Section 87207 provides that a public Official must
disclose the name and address of each souce of gifts of $50 or
more in value,
the amount and the date on which the gift
W&S
In our telephone conversat
of May 28,
1996,
You modified the
facts of Your orig±fl letter which included a gift of an
automobile made by Mr.
Servidio to the director
You corrected
the facts and indicated that in fact Mr.
Servidio had not given
the director a one_half interest in the automobile, but the
Mr.
Servidjo
director gave the One-half interest in the automobile to


Our File No.
A-96-155
Page
3
received,
and that the official must provide a general description
of the business activity of the donor.
o
Second,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official at any
level of state or local government from making, participating in
making or in any way attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official knows or has reason to know the official has a financial
interest.
(Section 87100.)
An official has a financial interest
in a decision within the meaniig of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally,
on any donor of,
or any intermediary or agent for a
donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating $280 or more in value
provided to,
received by,
or promised to the public official
within
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(Section 87103 (e) .)
o
And finally,
the Act now provides gift limit and honoraria
ban for local designated employees.
Section 89503(c) provides:
No member of a state board or commission or
designated employee of a state or local government
agency shall accept gifts from any single source in
any calendar year with a total value of more than
two hundred fifty
($250)
if the member or employee
would be required to report the receipt of income
or gifts from that source on his or her statement
of economic interests.
3
The Act also prohibits public officials from receiving
honoraria.
(Section 89502(c).)
Gifts in a Bona Fide Dating Relationship
Section 82028(a)
defines a “gift” as:
[Amy payment to the extent that consideration
of egual or greater value is not received and
includes a rebate or discount in the price of
anything of value unless the rebate or discount is
made in the regular course of business to members
of the public without regard to official status.
Any person,
other than a defendant in a criminal
action,
who claims that a payment is not a gift by
reason of receipt of consideration has the burden
The gift limit is adjusted biennially.
(Section 89503(f).)
The current limit is $280.


Our File No.
A-96.-155
Page
4
of proving that the consideration received is of
equal or greater value.
(Emphasis added.)
Section 82028(b)
sets out certain payments that are not
Considered gifts under the Act.
For example,
Section 82028
provides that gifts from certain relatives and that gifts from an
individual’s spouse are not reportable.
(Section 82028(b) (3),)
In considering these exceptions,
in the
Advice Letter,
No. A-89-105
we stated:
No section of the Act expressly provides a
disclosure exemption for persons living together or
dating.
The exemption was established because it
was believed to be consistent with the general
Policies of the Act.
These policies are found in
the Act’s definition of gift and in Commission
Opinions.
The Commission has consistently advised that
gifts received within the context of an established
“bona fide dating relationshipu are not prohibited
and need not be disclosed.
In such a relationship,
truly personal gifts such as gifts of
entertainment,
meals, personal property or expenses
involved in recreational travel are not prohibited
and need not be disclosed on an official’s state
ment of economic interest.
The Commission has
determined that
ITsuch a relationship is similar to
a family or spousal relationship,
in which personal
gifts are frequently exchanged,
and in which
disclosure of the gifts would not further the
purpose of the Act.”
(Shea Advice Letter,
No.
A-
84-085,
copy enclosed.)
It follows from the above that truly personal
gifts that you receive within the context of a
dating relationship are not reportable and create
no conflicts for you under the facts as discussed
above.
Thus,
the payments
yOU describe
(for the ring and the shared
food,
shelter,
and electricity)
would not be considered gifts for


Our File No.
A-96--155
Page5
purposes of the Act’s conflict of interest disclosure and
disqualification provisions and the gift limits of the Act.
4
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free tà contact
me at
(916)
322-566O.
Sincerely,
teven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
Please note that the Roff Advice Letter also cautioned that:
“gifts which are not truly personal in nature but are primarily
related to business activities
(such as money to be used for the
official’s business or to purchase business property)
must be
disclosed on the official’s statement of economic interest even if
they are from a person with whom the official has a bona fide
dating relationship.”
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of lO per page.
